sanskrit_devanagari:
कामक्रोधविमुक्तानां यतीनां यतचेतसाम् ।
अभितो ब्रह्मनिर्वाणं वर्तते विदितात्मनाम् ॥ २६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
kāma-krodha-vimuktānāṁ yatīnāṁ yata-cetasām |
abhito brahma-nirvāṇaṁ vartate viditātmanām || 26 ||

anvaya.meaning: cetasām — who have full control over the mind
ātmanām — of those who are self-realized

translations.verbatim_quote: Those who are free from anger and all material desires, who are self-realized, self-disciplined and constantly endeavoring for perfection, are assured of liberation in the Supreme in the very near future.

Those who are free from anger and all material desires, who are self-realized, self-disciplined and constantly endeavoring for perfection, are assured of liberation in the Supreme in the very near future.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1973 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Of the saintly persons who are constantly engaged in striving toward salvation, one who is in Kṛṣṇa consciousness is the best of all. The Bhāgavatam (4.22.39) confirms this fact as follows:yat-pāda-paṅkaja-palāśa-vilāsa-bhaktyākarmāśayaṁ grathitam udgrathayanti santaḥtadvan na rikta-matayo yatayo ’pi ruddha-sroto-gaṇās

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.26 To those who are free from desire and wrath; ‘who are wont to exert themselves’ i.e., who are practising self-control; whose ‘thought is controlled,’ i.e., whose minds are subdued; ‘who have conquered them,’ i.e., whose minds are under their control — to such persons the beatitude of the Brahma...

positions.rendering: everywhere; the state surrounds them on all sides — neither distant nor postponed
near, on all sides; the state is at hand for the disciplined practitioner

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three readings emphasise the immediacy of the state. The verse refuses the deferred-liberation reading. The metaphysical specification (what the state is) diverges along sectarian lines, but the immediacy is preserved.

literal_meaning: For renunciates (yatīnām) freed from desire-and-anger, with subdued minds, who have realised the self — brahma-nirvāṇa is near (abhitaḥ vartate), surrounding them on all sides.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse closes the sequence (5.24-5.26) with abhitaḥ — 'on all sides', 'near, surrounding'. Brahma-nirvāṇa is not far from the renunciate freed from desire-and-anger with subdued mind and self-realisation. The intimacy of the state (abhitaḥ) is structurally significant. Shankara, Ramanuja, and Prabhupada all read this as confirming the achievability of the state for the disciplined practitioner.
